ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for salak in current release; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of salak is 1–2 fruits (approximately 60 g edible flesh), consumed fresh as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Salak, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, iron) taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for salak, raw. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sugar, sodium, potassium) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry for salak (FDC ID 168153) where HPB does not report those fields; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous tropical fruit estimate for salak based on ripe tropical fruit cluster (GI 50–60)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for salak (Salacca zalacca) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the analogous ripe tropical fruit cluster (lychee GI 57, longan GI 55, rambutan GI 55) from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 8.52) / 100 ≈ 4.7 for a 60 g serving (14.2 g carb/100 g × 0.6 × available fraction ~0.87 after fibre). Note: GI 55 is considered low GI according to University of Sydney GI Database classification (low < 55, medium 55-69, high > 70). The previous iteration incorrectly classified 55 as medium.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (~256 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary targets.
hypertension.guidance: Salak is suitable for individuals with hypertension; limit to 1–2 fruits per serving to keep cumulative sugar intake within DASH guidelines, and prefer fresh over syrup-preserved forms which add sodium and sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 55 (low) and sugar content ~10 g/100 g; dietary fibre ~2.8 g/100 g partially moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may include salak in moderate portions (1–2 fruits, ~60 g) as part of a balanced meal; monitor individual blood glucose response and avoid consuming alongside other high-glycaemic foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful potassium (~256 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (~2.8 g/100 g) support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salak is a heart-friendly fruit choice; its low fat, low sodium, and moderate fibre content are consistent with cardioprotective dietary patterns, and it may be consumed regularly as part of a fruit-inclusive diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, >3 g whole-food fibre, vitamin D); polyphenol content is modest and unquantified.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salak is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis but does not provide sufficient anti-inflammatory factors to be recommended specifically; consume fresh rather than syrup-preserved forms and pair with omega-3-rich foods for better anti-inflammatory dietary balance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salak (Salacca zalacca), commonly known as snake fruit due to its distinctive reddish-brown scaly skin, is a tropical palm fruit native to Indonesia and widely consumed across Southeast Asia. The edible flesh is ivory-white, divided into lobes, with a crisp, crunchy texture and a complex sweet-acidic flavour profile reminiscent of a combination of apple, pineapple, and citrus. Each fruit weighs approximately 30–80 g and is typically eaten fresh as a snack, though it is also used in fruit salads, pickles, and traditional Indonesian sweets. Nutritionally, salak provides approximately 60 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (14.2 g/100 g), modest protein (0.5 g/100 g), and negligible fat (0.1 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is estimated at ~2.8 g/100 g, contributing to satiety and moderate glycaemic modulation. Potassium content is notable at ~256 mg/100 g, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Sodium is negligible (~2 mg/100 g), making salak compatible with low-sodium dietary patterns such as DASH. The estimated glycemic index is 55 (low), placing it in the low-GI category; glycemic load per 60 g serving is approximately 4.7 (low). Salak is suitable for most healthy adults, individuals with hypertension, and those with type 2 diabetes in controlled portions. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. In Singapore and Malaysia, salak is available in wet markets and supermarkets, often sold by the bunch. It is a culturally significant fruit in Indonesia, where it is also used in traditional medicine.
common_user_queries: Can I eat salak if I have high blood pressure?
Is salak the same as snake fruit?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input record). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sugar, sodium, potassium) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168153 for salak, raw. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database cluster for comparable ripe tropical fruits; this is documented. Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for GI estimate, yielding a composite high confidence rating.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database as reported in the input record (confidence 0.95). 2. Secondary nutrients dietary_fibre_g (~2.8 g), sugar_g (~10 g), sodium_mg (~2 mg), potassium_mg (~256 mg), vitamin_c_mg (~2 mg), and water_g (~82 g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168153 (Salak, raw) as HPB does not publish these fields for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for salak not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for salak in either HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central with sufficient confidence. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on comparable ripe tropical fruits (lychee GI 57, longan GI 55, rambutan GI 55) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for salak exists in the approved GI database as of April 2026. GI 55 is classified as low GI. 6. saturated_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for salak, raw). trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (naturally zero for a whole fresh fruit). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero for a plant food). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero for a plant food). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero for a fresh fruit). 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is the best available match for salak whole fruit; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible tropical fruit code — reviewers should verify against the current FoodEx2 catalogue. SNOMED CT code not identified in current release.
